NAMES THEMSELVES SOUND wine. The names themselves sound like one-word poems to me. Now they ITS NAME TO A WINE. THE and finally Shiraz, where I look for vineyards, knowing it gave its name to a mountains, magic-sounding Isfahan sitting on the Zayandeh River plateau VINEYARDS, KNOWING IT GAVE peaks, Tehran’s Alborz mountains and, beyond, the Caspian coast, the Zagros SHIRAZ, WHERE I LOOK FOR the world, my heart races faster as I look for Tabriz surrounded by volcanic RIVER PLATEAU AND FINALLY I was a boy on my first flights gazing at the geography in order to understand SITTING ON THE ZAYANDEH back to Abu Dhabi, where I spend some months in autumn. Just like when MAGIC-SOUNDING ISFAHAN I always gaze longingly at Iran from the window seat of a plane taking me THE ZAGROS MOUNTAINS, 1979 to something that felt more continuous with the identity of a civilisation.

BEYOND, THE CASPIAN COAST, rejecting identification with the Islamic Republic, trying to reach back before cal intent. Many of them now declare “I’m Persian” rather than “I’m Iranian”, ALBORZ MOUNTAINS AND, orbited back to using “Persia” and “Persian” with deliberate nostalgia and politi- VOLCANIC PEAKS, TEHRAN’S “Iran”. Now the Pahlavi descendants’ circles, along with some other exiles, have FOR TABRIZ SURROUNDED BY that dated definition of “Persia”, insisting everyone should switch to calling it MY HEART RACES AS I LOOK Shah himself officially requested the international community to stop using words, creating a homeland of poetry, has a paradox tied to its name. In 1935, the poetic irony in the fact that a country which has given so much importance to the people of the province of Fars as “Persis”. Iranians have always called their country “the land of the Aryans”—Iran. The very word is how foreigners, especially Europeans, have called it since the Greeks defined paradise for three religions. This is not ‘about’ Iran. It’s ‘around’ Persia.
